text_summary: KEYNOTE-158 is a multi-cohort, non-randomized phase 2 trial evaluating pembrolizumab monotherapy across 10 previously treated, advanced solid tumor types with TMB-High status (>=10 mutations/Mb). The trial established TMB-H as a pan-cancer predictive biomarker for immune checkpoint inhibitor response, with ORR of 29% in TMB-H versus 6% in TMB-low patients across all cohorts. This evidence led to the FDA approval of pembrolizumab for TMB-H solid tumors regardless of histology. The trial enrolled over 1,000 patients across cohorts including cervical, endometrial, biliary, SCLC, mesothelioma, neuroendocrine, and salivary gland cancers. TMB was assessed using FoundationOne CDx. The trial provided the second tissue-agnostic biomarker indication for pembrolizumab after MSI-H/dMMR.
title: Study of Pembrolizumab in Participants With Advanced Solid Tumors (KEYNOTE-158)

text_summary: FIGHT-202 is a phase 2, open-label, multicenter study of pemigatinib in previously treated, locally advanced or metastatic cholangiocarcinoma stratified by FGFR status: Cohort A (FGFR2 fusions/rearrangements), Cohort B (other FGF/FGFR alterations), and Cohort C (no FGF/FGFR alterations). In Cohort A, pemigatinib demonstrated ORR of 36%, median DOR of 9.1 months, and median PFS of 6.9 months. This pivotal trial led to FDA accelerated approval of pemigatinib for FGFR2 fusion-positive cholangiocarcinoma after at least one prior line of therapy. The trial demonstrated the importance of biomarker selection, as Cohorts B and C showed minimal activity. FGFR2 fusions were identified by NGS. Hyperphosphatemia was the most common adverse event due to on-target FGF23/FGFR1 inhibition.
title: A Study to Evaluate the Efficacy and Safety of Pemigatinib in Subjects With Previously Treated Cholangiocarcinoma (FIGHT-202)

text_summary: NCI-MATCH (Molecular Analysis for Therapy Choice) is a precision medicine clinical trial sponsored by the NCI that assigns treatment based on genomic alterations rather than cancer type. This umbrella/basket trial enrolled over 6,000 patients with refractory solid tumors, lymphomas, and multiple myeloma across approximately 40 treatment arms targeting specific molecular alterations including BRAF V600E, PIK3CA, HER2 amplification, FGFR amplification, MET amplification, CDK4/6 amplification, AKT mutations, and others. Each treatment arm functions as an independent phase 2 study with its own primary endpoint. NCI-MATCH demonstrated the feasibility of large-scale molecular screening in community oncology settings, with approximately 37% of screened patients having an actionable alteration. The trial has provided valuable data on the prevalence and clinical actionability of molecular targets across diverse tumor types.
title: NCI-MATCH: Targeted Therapy Directed by Genetic Testing in Treating Patients With Advanced Refractory Solid Tumors, Lymphomas, or Multiple Myeloma
biomarker_criteria: Multiple arms: BRAF V600E, PIK3CA, HER2 amplification, FGFR, MET, CDK4/6, AKT, and others

text_summary: MyPathway is a multi-center, non-randomized, open-label phase 2a basket trial evaluating HER2-directed therapy (trastuzumab plus pertuzumab), BRAF-directed therapy (vemurafenib), Hedgehog pathway inhibitor (vismodegib), and EGFR-directed therapy in patients with advanced solid tumors harboring specific molecular alterations outside of their approved indications. Key findings include activity of trastuzumab plus pertuzumab in HER2-amplified colorectal cancer (ORR 32%), HER2-mutant NSCLC (ORR 21%), and HER2-amplified bladder cancer (ORR 33%). The trial demonstrated the concept of biomarker-driven tumor-agnostic treatment and provided evidence supporting the expansion of targeted therapy indications beyond the originally approved cancer types. MyPathway has enrolled over 700 patients and continues to add cohorts for emerging targets.
title: MyPathway: An Open-Label Phase 2a Basket Study of Targeted Therapies in Advanced Solid Tumors
biomarker_criteria: HER2 amplification/mutation, BRAF V600E, Hedgehog pathway alteration, EGFR amplification

text_summary: STARTRK-2 is a global, phase 2 basket study evaluating entrectinib in patients with solid tumors harboring NTRK1/2/3, ROS1, or ALK gene fusions. The NTRK fusion cohort demonstrated ORR of 57% across 10+ tumor types with durable responses and intracranial activity (intracranial ORR 50%). The ROS1-positive NSCLC cohort showed ORR of 67% with median PFS of 15.7 months. Entrectinib demonstrated clinically meaningful CNS activity due to its ability to cross the blood-brain barrier. These results, combined with STARTRK-1 and ALKA-372-001 data, led to FDA approval of entrectinib for NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors and ROS1-positive NSCLC. The trial established entrectinib as a tissue-agnostic therapy and demonstrated the feasibility of multi-histology basket trial designs for rare genomic alterations.
title: Basket Study of Entrectinib (RXDX-101) for the Treatment of Patients With Solid Tumors Harboring NTRK 1/2/3, ROS1, or ALK Gene Rearrangements (STARTRK-2)
biomarker_criteria: NTRK1/2/3 fusion, ROS1 fusion, ALK fusion

text_summary: LIBRETTO-001 is a phase 1/2, multicenter, open-label study of selpercatinib in patients with RET-altered solid tumors including RET fusion-positive NSCLC, RET-mutant medullary thyroid cancer, and other RET-altered tumors. In previously treated RET fusion-positive NSCLC, selpercatinib achieved ORR of 64% and median PFS of 16.5 months. In treatment-naive NSCLC, ORR was 84%. For RET-mutant MTC, ORR was 73% in cabozantinib/vandetanib-pretreated patients and 92% in treatment-naive patients. The trial demonstrated robust intracranial activity and a manageable safety profile. Key adverse effects included dry mouth, hypertension, and elevated liver enzymes. LIBRETTO-001 led to FDA approval of selpercatinib across multiple RET-driven indications and established the paradigm of selective RET inhibition as superior to multikinase inhibitors.
title: Phase 1/2 Study of LOXO-292 in Patients With Advanced Solid Tumors, RET Fusion-Positive Solid Tumors, and Medullary Thyroid Cancer (LIBRETTO-001)
biomarker_criteria: RET fusion or RET activating mutation

text_summary: GEOMETRY mono-1 is a multi-center, non-randomized, open-label, multi-cohort phase 2 study evaluating capmatinib in advanced NSCLC with MET alterations. In patients with MET exon 14 skipping mutations, capmatinib demonstrated ORR of 68% in treatment-naive patients and 41% in previously treated patients. In patients with MET amplification (gene copy number >=10), ORR was 29% as second-line or beyond. The trial established MET exon 14 skipping as a distinct actionable driver in NSCLC and led to FDA approval of capmatinib. Capmatinib showed intracranial activity with intracranial ORR of 54% in patients with brain metastases. MET exon 14 skipping mutations are found in approximately 3-4% of NSCLC, predominantly in older patients with sarcomatoid or adenocarcinoma histology.
title: Study of Capmatinib in Patients With MET Exon 14 Skipping Mutation or MET Amplification Advanced Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (GEOMETRY mono-1)
biomarker_criteria: MET exon 14 skipping mutation or MET amplification (GCN>=10)

text_summary: DESTINY-Breast04 is a landmark phase 3 randomized controlled trial evaluating trastuzumab deruxtecan versus physician's choice chemotherapy in patients with HER2-low (IHC 1+ or IHC 2+/FISH-negative) metastatic breast cancer after 1-2 prior chemotherapy lines. T-DXd demonstrated significantly improved PFS (10.1 vs 5.4 months, HR 0.51) and OS (23.9 vs 17.5 months, HR 0.64) in the HR-positive cohort. In the overall population including TNBC with HER2-low expression, similar benefit was observed. This trial redefined the actionable HER2 landscape by establishing HER2-low as a clinically meaningful category, expanding the HER2-directed therapy eligible population to approximately 55% of breast cancer patients. The results led to FDA approval of T-DXd for HER2-low breast cancer. The bystander killing effect of the DXd payload enables activity in heterogeneous tumors.
title: Trastuzumab Deruxtecan Versus Investigator's Choice Chemotherapy in HER2-low Breast Cancer (DESTINY-Breast04)
interventions: trastuzumab deruxtecan vs physician's choice chemotherapy

text_summary: CodeBreaK 100 is a first-in-human phase 1/2 study of sotorasib (AMG 510) in patients with KRAS G12C-mutant advanced solid tumors. In the phase 2 NSCLC cohort (126 patients), sotorasib demonstrated ORR of 37.1%, median PFS of 6.8 months, and median OS of 12.5 months. The drug was well tolerated with grade 3+ adverse events in 21% of patients, most commonly diarrhea and hepatotoxicity. CodeBreaK 100 represented a historic milestone as the first clinical evidence that KRAS, the most commonly mutated oncogene previously considered undruggable, could be therapeutically targeted. The trial led to FDA accelerated approval of sotorasib for KRAS G12C-mutant NSCLC after prior systemic therapy. Responses were seen across diverse co-mutation profiles including STK11, KEAP1, and TP53. KRAS G12C is found in approximately 13% of NSCLC and 3% of CRC.
title: A Phase 1/2, Study Evaluating the Safety, Tolerability, PK, and Efficacy of AMG 510 (Sotorasib) in KRAS G12C Mutant Solid Tumors (CodeBreaK 100)

text_summary: CROWN is a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing lorlatinib versus crizotinib as first-line therapy for ALK-positive advanced NSCLC. Lorlatinib demonstrated a dramatically superior 3-year PFS rate of 64% versus 19% for crizotinib, with median PFS not reached for lorlatinib at 36 months (compared to 9.3 months for crizotinib). The intracranial ORR for lorlatinib was 82% versus 23% for crizotinib, demonstrating exceptional CNS activity. The updated 5-year analysis continued to show sustained PFS benefit. CROWN established lorlatinib as a first-line option for ALK-positive NSCLC alongside alectinib. Key adverse effects included hypercholesterolemia, hypertriglyceridemia, weight gain, and CNS effects. The trial demonstrated that upfront use of the most potent ALK inhibitor may delay or prevent resistance mutations, potentially improving long-term outcomes compared to sequential TKI strategies.
title: A Study Comparing Lorlatinib With Crizotinib in First Line Treatment of Patients With ALK-Positive NSCLC (CROWN)

text_summary: ALEX is a pivotal phase 3 randomized trial comparing alectinib versus crizotinib as first-line therapy for ALK-positive advanced NSCLC. Alectinib demonstrated superior PFS of 34.8 months versus 10.9 months for crizotinib (HR 0.43), and superior CNS activity with intracranial ORR of 81% for alectinib. The 5-year OS rates were 62.5% for alectinib versus 45.5% for crizotinib. ALEX established alectinib as the NCCN-preferred first-line ALK inhibitor and set a new benchmark for molecularly targeted therapy in NSCLC. The trial demonstrated that alectinib's superior CNS penetration translates into reduced incidence of brain metastases as site of progression. Alectinib was well tolerated with lower rates of gastrointestinal toxicity compared to crizotinib. The results fundamentally changed the standard of care for ALK-positive NSCLC worldwide.
title: A Study Comparing Alectinib With Crizotinib in Treatment-Naive Anaplastic Lymphoma Kinase-Positive Advanced Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (ALEX)

text_summary: SOLO-1 is a phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of olaparib maintenance therapy in patients with newly diagnosed, advanced BRCA1/2-mutant ovarian cancer who had a complete or partial response to first-line platinum-based chemotherapy. Olaparib dramatically improved median PFS to 56 months versus 13.8 months for placebo (HR 0.33), representing the longest PFS ever reported in a randomized phase 3 ovarian cancer trial. The 7-year follow-up showed sustained DFS benefit with 67% of olaparib patients disease-free at 2 years post-treatment versus 32% for placebo. SOLO-1 established PARP inhibitor maintenance as standard of care for BRCA-mutant ovarian cancer and demonstrated that early intervention with PARP inhibitors can fundamentally alter the disease trajectory. The trial included both germline and somatic BRCA1/2 mutations.
title: Olaparib Maintenance Monotherapy in Patients With BRCA Mutated Ovarian Cancer (SOLO-1)

text_summary: ARIEL3 is a phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of rucaparib maintenance therapy in patients with platinum-sensitive, recurrent ovarian cancer. The trial enrolled patients across three biomarker groups: BRCA-mutant, HRD-positive (BRCA wild-type but high LOH), and all-comers. In the BRCA-mutant subgroup, rucaparib maintenance improved PFS to 16.6 months versus 5.4 months for placebo. The HRD-positive non-BRCA subgroup also showed benefit (PFS 9.7 vs 5.4 months), establishing HRD as a predictive biomarker beyond BRCA mutations. The all-comers population also benefited (PFS 10.8 vs 5.4 months). ARIEL3 validated the foundation assay for LOH-based HRD testing and demonstrated that rucaparib benefit extends beyond BRCA-mutant tumors to the broader HRD-positive population. Key adverse effects included anemia, fatigue, and nausea.
title: A Study of Rucaparib as Switch Maintenance Following Platinum-Based Chemotherapy in Patients With Ovarian Cancer (ARIEL3)

text_summary: CheckMate-227 is a phase 3, open-label, randomized trial evaluating nivolumab-based regimens versus chemotherapy as first-line therapy for advanced NSCLC. Part 1a demonstrated that nivolumab plus ipilimumab significantly improved OS versus chemotherapy in PD-L1>=1% patients (17.1 vs 14.9 months, HR 0.79). In a pre-specified analysis, the benefit was particularly pronounced in TMB>=10 mut/Mb patients (PFS 7.2 vs 5.5 months). Part 1b showed benefit in PD-L1<1% patients treated with nivolumab plus ipilimumab plus limited chemotherapy. The trial established dual checkpoint blockade (PD-1 plus CTLA-4) as an effective immunotherapy backbone in NSCLC and informed the design of CheckMate-9LA. However, the TMB biomarker findings were controversial and not included in the FDA approval. The trial highlighted the complexity of biomarker selection in immunotherapy and the potential for PD-L1-independent immune activity with combination approaches.
title: An Investigational Immuno-therapy Study of Nivolumab, or Nivolumab Plus Ipilimumab in Advanced Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (CheckMate-227)

text_summary: IMpower150 is a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial evaluating atezolizumab plus bevacizumab plus carboplatin/paclitaxel (ABCP) versus bevacizumab plus chemotherapy (BCP) in first-line non-squamous advanced NSCLC. ABCP demonstrated improved OS (19.2 vs 14.7 months, HR 0.78) and PFS (8.3 vs 6.8 months, HR 0.62). Notably, IMpower150 included patients with EGFR mutations and ALK rearrangements who had progressed on TKI therapy, a population typically excluded from immunotherapy trials. The EGFR/ALK-positive subgroup showed PFS benefit with ABCP. The trial also demonstrated benefit across PD-L1 subgroups and in patients with liver metastases. The addition of bevacizumab to immunotherapy plus chemotherapy is hypothesized to enhance anti-tumor immunity through VEGF-mediated immunosuppression reversal. IMpower150 provided a unique therapeutic option for patients with EGFR/ALK-positive NSCLC post-TKI.
title: A Study of Atezolizumab in Combination With Bevacizumab and Chemotherapy in Participants With Non-Squamous Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (IMpower150)
interventions: atezolizumab + bevacizumab + chemotherapy vs bevacizumab + chemotherapy

text_summary: COMBI-d is a phase 3, randomized, double-blind trial comparing dabrafenib plus trametinib versus dabrafenib plus placebo in patients with BRAF V600E/K-mutant unresectable or metastatic melanoma. The combination demonstrated superior PFS (11.0 vs 8.8 months), OS (25.1 vs 18.7 months), and ORR (69% vs 53%). The landmark 5-year analysis showed OS rate of 34% for the combination, unprecedented for metastatic melanoma at that time. The combination reduced the incidence of cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (2% vs 9%) compared to BRAF inhibitor monotherapy by blocking paradoxical ERK activation. COMBI-d, along with COMBI-v, established dabrafenib plus trametinib as the standard BRAF+MEK inhibitor combination for BRAF-mutant melanoma. Pyrexia was the most distinctive adverse event of dabrafenib (occurring in ~50% of patients). The trial demonstrated that vertical pathway blockade improves both efficacy and tolerability.
title: A Study Comparing the Combination of Dabrafenib and Trametinib With Dabrafenib and Placebo in BRAF V600E/K Melanoma (COMBI-d)

text_summary: BEACON CRC is a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial evaluating encorafenib plus cetuximab (doublet) and encorafenib plus binimetinib plus cetuximab (triplet) versus investigators' choice chemotherapy in BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic colorectal cancer after one or two prior therapies. The triplet combination demonstrated OS of 9.3 months versus 5.9 months for control (HR 0.52) and ORR of 27% versus 2%. The doublet (encorafenib plus cetuximab) showed similar efficacy and became the preferred regimen due to better tolerability. BEACON CRC established that BRAF V600E-mutant CRC requires concurrent EGFR inhibition to prevent adaptive feedback activation, unlike melanoma where BRAF+MEK is sufficient. This trial transformed the treatment of BRAF V600E-mutant CRC, which carries the worst prognosis among CRC molecular subtypes with median OS of approximately 12 months from diagnosis of metastatic disease.
title: Encorafenib Plus Cetuximab With or Without Binimetinib vs. Irinotecan-Based Chemotherapy in BRAF V600E-Mutant Metastatic CRC (BEACON CRC)

text_summary: ATLAS is a multi-center, observational, prospective molecular screening study that profiles patients with advanced solid tumors using comprehensive genomic profiling to identify actionable molecular alterations and match patients to biomarker-driven clinical trials. The study utilizes next-generation sequencing, whole-exome sequencing, and transcriptomic analysis to characterize the molecular landscape of refractory cancers. ATLAS aims to demonstrate that molecular profiling in the real-world setting can identify actionable targets in a significant proportion of patients and improve clinical outcomes through biomarker-driven therapy selection. The study collects longitudinal data on molecular evolution, treatment outcomes, and resistance mechanisms, creating a living molecular database for precision oncology research. ATLAS has screened thousands of patients and served as a feeder study for multiple interventional biomarker-driven trials.
title: The ATLAS Study: A Multi-Center Molecular Screening Study for Advanced Solid Tumors
biomarker_criteria: comprehensive genomic profiling for actionable alterations

text_summary: TAPISTRY is a phase 2, global, open-label, multi-cohort platform trial evaluating targeted therapies in patients with solid tumors harboring specific oncogenic driver alterations identified by NGS. Cohorts include ROS1 fusion (entrectinib), ALK-positive (alectinib), AKT-mutant (ipatasertib), HER2-mutant (trastuzumab emtansine), NTRK1/2/3 fusion (entrectinib), and other driver cohorts. The platform design allows rapid addition of new cohorts as novel targets emerge. TAPISTRY tests the hypothesis that genomic driver matching can produce meaningful clinical benefit across diverse histologies. The trial uses centralized molecular screening via FoundationOne CDx and requires patients to have exhausted standard-of-care options. TAPISTRY represents the evolution of basket trial designs toward more efficient platform protocols and has contributed data on the prevalence and clinical actionability of rare driver alterations across tumor types.
title: A Study Evaluating Targeted Therapies in Patients With Solid Tumors With Specific Genomic Alterations (TAPISTRY)
biomarker_criteria: ROS1, ALK, AKT, HER2 mutation, NTRK, and other specific genomic alterations
interventions: entrectinib, alectinib, ipatasertib, trastuzumab emtansine, and others

text_summary: TAPUR (Targeted Agent and Profiling Utilization Registry) is an ASCO-sponsored phase 2 basket study matching FDA-approved targeted therapies to patients with advanced cancers harboring genomic alterations known to be drug targets, used outside of their currently approved indications. The study includes multiple cohorts with drugs including pembrolizumab, olaparib, trastuzumab, sunitinib, palbociclib, osimertinib, and others matched to molecular alterations. TAPUR operates pragmatically in community oncology settings and has enrolled over 4,000 patients across 1,500+ sites. Key findings include activity of pembrolizumab in TMB-high cancers across multiple histologies, palbociclib in CDKN2A-altered tumors, and sunitinib in KIT-mutant cancers. TAPUR generates real-world evidence on biomarker-drug combinations and has supported FDA labeling discussions. The trial demonstrates that precision medicine can be implemented at scale in community oncology practice.
title: Targeted Agent and Profiling Utilization Registry (TAPUR) Study
biomarker_criteria: Various genomic alterations matched to FDA-approved targeted agents
interventions: Multiple FDA-approved targeted agents used off-label

text_summary: ASCENT is a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing sacituzumab govitecan versus physician's choice single-agent chemotherapy (eribulin, vinorelbine, capecitabine, or gemcitabine) in patients with metastatic triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) who had received at least two prior systemic therapies. Sacituzumab govitecan demonstrated significantly improved PFS (5.6 vs 1.7 months, HR 0.41) and OS (12.1 vs 6.7 months, HR 0.48) in patients without brain metastases. The ORR was 35% for sacituzumab govitecan versus 5% for chemotherapy. The trial established sacituzumab govitecan as the new standard for pretreated metastatic TNBC, a population with historically poor prognosis and limited therapeutic options. Key grade 3+ adverse events included neutropenia (51%), diarrhea (10%), and febrile neutropenia (6%). The trial led to full FDA approval of sacituzumab govitecan for metastatic TNBC after at least two prior therapies.
title: A Study of Sacituzumab Govitecan in Refractory/Relapsed Triple-Negative Breast Cancer (ASCENT)
interventions: sacituzumab govitecan vs physician's choice chemotherapy

text_summary: EV-302/KEYNOTE-A39 is a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing enfortumab vedotin plus pembrolizumab versus gemcitabine plus platinum-based chemotherapy as first-line treatment for locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma. The combination demonstrated significantly improved PFS (12.5 vs 6.3 months, HR 0.45) and OS (not reached vs 16.1 months, HR 0.47). The ORR was 68% for enfortumab vedotin plus pembrolizumab versus 44% for chemotherapy, with a CR rate of 29% versus 13%. This trial fundamentally changed the first-line treatment paradigm for advanced urothelial carcinoma, establishing an ADC-immunotherapy combination as the new standard of care. The benefit was consistent across PD-L1 subgroups and cisplatin-eligible/ineligible populations. Key adverse effects included skin reactions, peripheral neuropathy, and hyperglycemia.
title: Enfortumab Vedotin Plus Pembrolizumab vs Chemotherapy in Untreated Locally Advanced or Metastatic Urothelial Carcinoma (EV-302/KEYNOTE-A39)
interventions: enfortumab vedotin + pembrolizumab vs gemcitabine + platinum chemotherapy

text_summary: MIRASOL (also known as GOG-3045/ENGOT-ov55) is a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing mirvetuximab soravtansine versus investigator's choice chemotherapy (paclitaxel, pegylated liposomal doxorubicin, or topotecan) in patients with FRalpha-positive, platinum-resistant epithelial ovarian, fallopian tube, or primary peritoneal cancer. Mirvetuximab soravtansine demonstrated improved PFS (5.6 vs 4.0 months, HR 0.65) and, importantly, a statistically significant OS improvement (16.5 vs 12.8 months, HR 0.67). The ORR was 42.3% versus 15.9%. This confirmatory trial validated the accelerated approval from the SORAYA study and established mirvetuximab soravtansine as a new standard for FRalpha-positive platinum-resistant ovarian cancer. The VENTANA FOLR1 (26B3) RxDx companion diagnostic was used for patient selection (PS2+ score). Ocular toxicity required monitoring with regular ophthalmologic exams.
title: Mirvetuximab Soravtansine vs Chemotherapy in FRalpha-Positive, Platinum-Resistant Ovarian Cancer (MIRASOL)
interventions: mirvetuximab soravtansine vs investigator's choice chemotherapy

text_summary: SPOTLIGHT is a phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial evaluating zolbetuximab plus mFOLFOX6 versus placebo plus mFOLFOX6 in patients with CLDN18.2-positive, HER2-negative, locally advanced unresectable or metastatic gastric or gastroesophageal junction adenocarcinoma. The addition of zolbetuximab significantly improved PFS (10.6 vs 8.7 months, HR 0.75) and OS (18.2 vs 15.5 months, HR 0.75). CLDN18.2 positivity was defined as moderate-to-strong membrane staining in >=75% of tumor cells by the VENTANA CLDN18 (43-14A) IHC assay. SPOTLIGHT, along with the complementary GLOW trial (zolbetuximab + CAPOX), established zolbetuximab as a new targeted therapy for CLDN18.2-positive gastric cancer. Key adverse effects were primarily gastrointestinal (nausea 76%, vomiting 67%), most pronounced during early infusion cycles. This trial represents the first successful targeting of a tight junction protein in oncology.
title: Zolbetuximab Plus mFOLFOX6 vs Placebo Plus mFOLFOX6 in CLDN18.2-Positive Gastric/GEJ Cancer (SPOTLIGHT)

text_summary: DeLLphi-301 is a phase 2, open-label, multicenter trial evaluating tarlatamab, a DLL3-targeting bispecific T-cell engager (BiTE), in patients with previously treated extensive-stage small cell lung cancer (ES-SCLC). The trial evaluated two dose levels (10mg and 100mg every 2 weeks). At the 10mg dose (the recommended dose), tarlatamab demonstrated ORR of 40%, median DOR of 9.7 months, and median OS of 14.3 months. The 100mg dose showed ORR of 32%. These results are notable given the historically poor outcomes for relapsed SCLC (median OS ~5-7 months with topotecan). The trial led to FDA accelerated approval of tarlatamab for ES-SCLC after prior platinum-based chemotherapy. Cytokine release syndrome occurred in 53% of patients (grade 3 in 1%), predominantly during cycle 1. ICANS occurred in 10% of patients. Step-up dosing and hospitalization for initial doses were implemented as safety measures.
title: Tarlatamab in Patients With Previously Treated Small Cell Lung Cancer (DeLLphi-301)
biomarker_criteria: None required (DLL3 expression near-universal in SCLC)

text_summary: This phase 2, open-label, multicenter study evaluated tazemetostat in patients with relapsed/refractory EZH2-mutant or EZH2 wild-type follicular lymphoma (FL) and in patients with INI1-negative (SMARCB1-loss) tumors including epithelioid sarcoma. In the EZH2-mutant FL cohort, tazemetostat demonstrated ORR of 69% with a CR rate of 12% and median DOR of 10.9 months. In EZH2 wild-type FL, ORR was 34%. In epithelioid sarcoma, ORR was 15% with durable responses exceeding 12 months. These results led to FDA accelerated approval of tazemetostat for both relapsed/refractory FL (after two prior systemic therapies) and metastatic/locally advanced epithelioid sarcoma. The drug demonstrated a favorable safety profile with the most common adverse events being fatigue (36%), nausea (24%), upper respiratory infection (21%), and musculoskeletal pain (20%). EZH2 mutation testing by the cobas EZH2 Mutation Test was used as a companion diagnostic for the FL indication.
title: A Phase II Study of Tazemetostat in Participants With Relapsed or Refractory B-cell Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma and Advanced Solid Tumors

text_summary: CheckMate-067 is a landmark phase 3, randomized, double-blind trial comparing nivolumab plus ipilimumab, nivolumab monotherapy, and ipilimumab monotherapy in treatment-naive patients with unresectable stage III or IV melanoma. At the 6.5-year follow-up, median OS was 72.1 months for nivolumab plus ipilimumab, 36.9 months for nivolumab alone, and 19.9 months for ipilimumab alone. The 5-year OS rates were 52%, 44%, and 26%, respectively. Nivolumab plus ipilimumab achieved ORR of 58% with a CR rate of 22%. Notably, BRAF mutation status did not significantly affect the magnitude of benefit from immunotherapy. Grade 3-4 treatment-related adverse events occurred in 59% of the combination arm, 23% with nivolumab alone, and 28% with ipilimumab alone. CheckMate-067 established dual checkpoint blockade as a reference regimen for advanced melanoma and informed the development of combination immunotherapy across multiple cancer types.
title: A Study of Nivolumab Plus Ipilimumab Versus Ipilimumab Alone in Previously Untreated Advanced Melanoma (CheckMate-067)
biomarker_criteria: All-comers (PD-L1 and BRAF status assessed but not used for selection)
interventions: nivolumab + ipilimumab vs nivolumab vs ipilimumab

text_summary: CAPItello-291 is a phase 3, randomized, double-blind trial comparing capivasertib plus fulvestrant versus placebo plus fulvestrant in patients with HR-positive/HER2-negative locally advanced or metastatic breast cancer after progression on aromatase inhibitor with or without CDK4/6 inhibitor. In the overall population, capivasertib plus fulvestrant improved PFS to 5.5 vs 3.6 months (HR 0.60). In the PIK3CA/AKT1/PTEN-altered population (~40% of enrolled patients), PFS was 7.3 vs 3.1 months (HR 0.50). ORR in the altered population was 28.8% vs 11.5%. CAPItello-291 led to FDA approval of capivasertib plus fulvestrant for patients with PIK3CA/AKT1/PTEN-altered disease. The FoundationOne CDx is the companion diagnostic. Key adverse effects included diarrhea (72%), rash (47%), and hyperglycemia (16%). Capivasertib is administered on an intermittent 4-days-on/3-days-off schedule to manage toxicity.
title: Capivasertib + Fulvestrant vs Placebo + Fulvestrant in HR+/HER2- Advanced Breast Cancer (CAPItello-291)

text_summary: EMERALD is a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing elacestrant versus investigator's choice standard-of-care endocrine monotherapy (fulvestrant or an aromatase inhibitor) in patients with ER-positive/HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer who had received one or two prior lines of endocrine therapy including a CDK4/6 inhibitor. In the ESR1-mutant population (approximately 48% of enrolled patients), elacestrant demonstrated PFS of 3.8 vs 1.9 months (HR 0.55). In the overall population, PFS was 2.8 vs 1.9 months (HR 0.70). The trial established ESR1 mutation as a predictive biomarker for oral SERD benefit and led to FDA approval of elacestrant for ESR1-mutant, ER-positive/HER2-negative advanced breast cancer. ESR1 mutations were detected by ctDNA (Guardant360 CDx). Key adverse effects included nausea (35%), fatigue (19%), vomiting (19%), and decreased appetite (15%). Elacestrant became the first oral SERD to reach approval, offering convenience over injectable fulvestrant.
title: Elacestrant vs Standard of Care for ER+/HER2- Advanced Breast Cancer (EMERALD)
biomarker_criteria: ESR1 mutation by ctDNA (Guardant360 CDx); or all-comers
interventions: elacestrant vs investigator's choice endocrine monotherapy

text_summary: TRIDENT-1 is a phase 1/2, multicenter, open-label trial evaluating repotrectinib in patients with ROS1-positive or NTRK-positive advanced solid tumors. In the ROS1-positive NSCLC TKI-naive cohort, repotrectinib demonstrated ORR of 79% with median DOR not reached. In patients previously treated with one ROS1 TKI, ORR was 38%, and specifically against the G2032R solvent-front mutation, ORR was 59%. These results led to FDA approval of repotrectinib for ROS1-positive NSCLC in both TKI-naive and previously treated settings. Repotrectinib's macrocyclic structure enables it to fit within the constrained binding pocket created by solvent-front mutations. The trial also showed activity in NTRK fusion-positive cancers. Key adverse effects included dizziness (58%), dysgeusia (50%), peripheral neuropathy (17%), and constipation (35%). CNS effects were generally mild and transient. TRIDENT-1 established repotrectinib as a next-generation option with unique resistance-mutation coverage.
title: A Study of Repotrectinib in Patients With Advanced Solid Tumors Harboring ROS1 or NTRK1-3 Rearrangements (TRIDENT-1)

text_summary: RATIFY (CALGB 10603) is a phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial evaluating midostaurin added to standard induction (daunorubicin plus cytarabine 7+3) and consolidation chemotherapy in 717 patients aged 18-59 years with newly diagnosed FLT3-mutant AML (ITD or TKD). Midostaurin significantly improved OS (HR 0.78, one-sided p=0.009) with median OS of 74.7 versus 25.6 months. The 4-year OS rate was 51.4% versus 44.3%. CR rates were similar between arms, but event-free survival favored midostaurin. Benefit was observed in both FLT3-ITD and FLT3-TKD subgroups. RATIFY established midostaurin plus chemotherapy as the standard first-line therapy for younger fit patients with FLT3-mutant AML and mandated FLT3 testing at diagnosis. Patients achieving CR could proceed to allogeneic stem cell transplant in first remission. The trial led to FDA approval of midostaurin in 2017.
title: Midostaurin or Placebo in Combination With Standard Chemotherapy in Patients With Newly Diagnosed FLT3-Mutated AML (RATIFY/CALGB 10603)
interventions: midostaurin + standard chemotherapy vs placebo + standard chemotherapy
sponsor: Novartis / Alliance for Clinical Trials in Oncology

text_summary: ADMIRAL is a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing gilteritinib, a selective oral FLT3 inhibitor, with salvage chemotherapy (MEC, FLAG-IDA, LDAC, or azacitidine) in 371 patients with relapsed or refractory FLT3-mutant (ITD or TKD) AML. Gilteritinib significantly improved OS (median 9.3 vs 5.6 months, HR 0.64, p<0.001). The CR+CRh rate was 34.0% versus 15.3%. Gilteritinib maintained activity against both FLT3-ITD and FLT3-TKD mutations and in patients who relapsed after allogeneic stem cell transplant. Key adverse events included febrile neutropenia (46%), anemia (41%), and differentiation syndrome (3%). ADMIRAL established gilteritinib as the standard of care for relapsed/refractory FLT3-mutant AML, providing a targeted oral alternative to intensive salvage chemotherapy regimens.
title: Study of ASP2215 (Gilteritinib) Versus Salvage Chemotherapy in Relapsed/Refractory FLT3-Mutated AML (ADMIRAL)

text_summary: AGILE is a phase 3, randomized, double-blind trial comparing ivosidenib plus azacitidine versus placebo plus azacitidine in 146 patients with newly diagnosed IDH1-mutant AML who were ineligible for intensive induction chemotherapy. Ivosidenib plus azacitidine demonstrated significant EFS benefit (HR 0.33, p=0.002), improved CR rate (47.2% vs 14.9%), and OS improvement (median 24.0 vs 7.9 months, HR 0.44, p=0.001). Molecular response with IDH1 mutation clearance was achieved in 36% of patients. Key adverse events included differentiation syndrome (14%) and QTc prolongation (10%). AGILE established ivosidenib plus azacitidine as the preferred first-line therapy for IDH1-mutant AML patients ineligible for intensive chemotherapy.
title: A Study of AG-120 (Ivosidenib) Plus Azacitidine Versus Placebo Plus Azacitidine in Patients With Previously Untreated IDH1-Mutated AML (AGILE)

text_summary: VIALE-A is a phase 3, randomized, double-blind trial comparing venetoclax plus azacitidine versus placebo plus azacitidine in 431 patients with newly diagnosed AML who were ineligible for intensive chemotherapy. The combination significantly improved OS (14.7 vs 9.6 months, HR 0.66, p<0.001) and CR+CRi rate (66.4% vs 28.3%). Benefit was observed across molecular subgroups including IDH1/2-mutant, FLT3-mutant, NPM1-mutant, and TP53-mutant AML, with the most pronounced benefit in IDH1/2-mutant patients. Hematologic toxicity was common (neutropenia 42%, thrombocytopenia 45%, febrile neutropenia 42%). VIALE-A established venetoclax plus azacitidine as the standard of care for older or unfit patients with AML, transforming outcomes in this historically difficult-to-treat population.
title: Venetoclax in Combination With Azacitidine Versus Azacitidine in Treatment-Naive Subjects With AML (VIALE-A)
biomarker_criteria: All-comers (benefit across IDH, FLT3, NPM1, TP53 subgroups)

text_summary: IRIS is a landmark phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing imatinib (400mg daily) with interferon-alpha plus low-dose cytarabine in 1,106 patients with newly diagnosed chronic-phase chronic myeloid leukemia (CML). At 18 months, imatinib demonstrated complete cytogenetic response rate of 76.2% versus 14.5% for interferon-based therapy. The 10-year follow-up showed estimated OS of 83.3% for imatinib. IRIS transformed CML from a fatal disease to a manageable chronic condition and established the paradigm of oncogene-addicted targeted therapy. BCR-ABL1 molecular monitoring by quantitative RT-PCR became the standard for treatment response assessment. The trial validated BCR-ABL1 as a therapeutic target and remains the foundation for TKI-based CML management.
title: Imatinib Mesylate Versus IFN-alpha Plus Cytarabine in Newly Diagnosed CML in Chronic Phase (IRIS)

text_summary: COMFORT-I is a phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial evaluating ruxolitinib, a JAK1/JAK2 inhibitor, in 309 patients with intermediate-2 or high-risk myelofibrosis (primary MF, post-PV MF, or post-ET MF). At 24 weeks, 41.9% of ruxolitinib patients achieved the primary endpoint of >=35% spleen volume reduction versus 0.7% for placebo. Ruxolitinib significantly improved symptom burden and quality of life. Long-term follow-up demonstrated OS benefit (median 5.3 vs 3.8 years, HR 0.69). Benefit was observed regardless of JAK2 V617F mutation status, though JAK2-mutant patients had numerically greater responses. Key adverse events included anemia and thrombocytopenia. COMFORT-I established ruxolitinib as the first approved therapy for myelofibrosis and validated JAK-STAT pathway targeting in myeloproliferative neoplasms.
title: A Study of INCB018424 in Patients With Primary Myelofibrosis, Post-Polycythemia Vera MF, or Post-Essential Thrombocythemia MF (COMFORT-I)
biomarker_criteria: JAK2 V617F (enriched but not required; includes CALR, MPL mutants)

text_summary: PROfound is a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing olaparib with physician's choice of enzalutamide or abiraterone in 387 patients with mCRPC harboring HRR gene alterations who had progressed on a prior novel hormonal agent. In Cohort A (BRCA1/2 or ATM, n=245), olaparib improved median PFS to 7.4 versus 3.6 months (HR 0.34, p<0.001) and OS to 19.1 versus 14.7 months (HR 0.69). The benefit was most pronounced in BRCA2-altered patients. Key adverse events included anemia (46%), nausea (41%), and fatigue (41%). PROfound established olaparib as the first PARP inhibitor approved for mCRPC and mandated HRR gene testing in advanced prostate cancer.
title: Olaparib vs Enzalutamide or Abiraterone in Men With mCRPC and HRR Gene Alterations (PROfound)
biomarker_criteria: BRCA1/2, ATM, or other HRR gene alterations

text_summary: VISION is a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial evaluating 177Lu-PSMA-617 plus standard of care versus standard of care alone in 831 patients with PSMA-positive mCRPC who had progressed after at least one androgen receptor pathway inhibitor and one or two taxane chemotherapy regimens. PSMA positivity was determined by 68Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT. 177Lu-PSMA-617 significantly improved PFS (8.7 vs 3.4 months, HR 0.40) and OS (15.3 vs 11.3 months, HR 0.62). PSA50 response rate was 46% versus 7%. Key adverse events included dry mouth, fatigue, and hematologic toxicity. VISION established radioligand therapy as a new treatment modality in prostate cancer and led to FDA approval of 177Lu-PSMA-617.
title: 177Lu-PSMA-617 in mCRPC (VISION)
interventions: 177Lu-PSMA-617 + standard of care vs standard of care

text_summary: IMbrave150 is a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing atezolizumab plus bevacizumab with sorafenib in 501 patients with unresectable HCC who had not received prior systemic therapy. The combination demonstrated improved OS (19.2 vs 13.4 months, HR 0.66), PFS (6.9 vs 4.3 months, HR 0.65), and ORR (30% vs 11%). The benefit was observed across etiologies (HBV, HCV, non-viral) and PD-L1 subgroups. Bevacizumab addresses VEGF-mediated immunosuppression in the hepatic microenvironment. Key adverse events included hypertension, proteinuria, and upper GI bleeding. Variceal screening and management are mandatory. IMbrave150 ended the sorafenib monotherapy era and established I/O-TKI combination as the first-line standard for advanced HCC.
title: A Study of Atezolizumab Plus Bevacizumab Versus Sorafenib in Patients With Unresectable HCC (IMbrave150)
biomarker_criteria: All-comers (Child-Pugh A, no prior systemic therapy)

text_summary: HIMALAYA is a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial evaluating the STRIDE regimen (single tremelimumab regular interval durvalumab) versus sorafenib in 1,171 patients with unresectable HCC without prior systemic therapy. STRIDE demonstrated superior OS (16.4 vs 13.8 months, HR 0.78, p=0.0035) with a 3-year OS rate of 30.7% versus 20.2%. ORR was 20.1% versus 5.1%. The single priming dose of anti-CTLA-4 was designed to enhance antitumor T-cell response while limiting toxicity. HIMALAYA established the STRIDE regimen as an alternative first-line option for HCC, particularly for patients ineligible for bevacizumab due to high variceal bleeding risk.
title: A Study of Durvalumab and Tremelimumab Versus Sorafenib in Patients With HCC (HIMALAYA)

text_summary: CheckMate-214 is a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing nivolumab plus ipilimumab with sunitinib in 1,096 patients with previously untreated advanced RCC. In intermediate/poor-risk patients (IMDC criteria), the combination demonstrated superior OS (47.0 vs 26.6 months at 5-year follow-up, HR 0.68), ORR (42% vs 27%), and CR rate (9% vs 1%). Durable complete responses were observed with some patients remaining in CR off all therapy. PD-L1>=1% patients derived the greatest benefit. In favorable-risk patients, sunitinib remained superior for PFS. CheckMate-214 established dual checkpoint blockade as the first-line standard for intermediate/poor-risk mRCC.
title: Nivolumab Plus Ipilimumab Versus Sunitinib in First-Line Treatment of Patients With Advanced RCC (CheckMate-214)
biomarker_criteria: IMDC intermediate/poor risk (benefit across PD-L1 subgroups)

text_summary: KEYNOTE-426 is a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing pembrolizumab plus axitinib with sunitinib in 861 patients with previously untreated advanced clear cell RCC across all IMDC risk groups. The combination improved OS (HR 0.68), PFS (15.4 vs 11.1 months, HR 0.69), and ORR (59.3% vs 35.7%). Unlike CheckMate-214, benefit was observed across all risk categories including favorable risk. KEYNOTE-426 established I/O plus TKI as a broadly applicable first-line option for mRCC.
title: Pembrolizumab Plus Axitinib Versus Sunitinib for Advanced RCC (KEYNOTE-426)
biomarker_criteria: All IMDC risk groups, all PD-L1 subgroups

text_summary: INVICTUS is a phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial evaluating ripretinib in 129 patients with advanced GIST who had received at least 3 prior kinase inhibitors (imatinib, sunitinib, regorafenib). Ripretinib significantly improved PFS (6.3 vs 1.0 months, HR 0.15) and demonstrated a trend toward improved OS (15.1 vs 6.6 months, HR 0.36). Ripretinib is a broad-spectrum switch-control KIT/PDGFRA inhibitor that covers both primary and secondary resistance mutations. Key adverse events included alopecia, fatigue, and palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia. INVICTUS established ripretinib as standard fourth-line therapy for advanced GIST.
title: Ripretinib vs Placebo in Patients With Advanced GIST Who Have Received at Least 3 Prior Kinase Inhibitors (INVICTUS)
biomarker_criteria: KIT or PDGFRA mutation (advanced GIST after 3+ prior TKIs)

text_summary: NAVIGATOR is a phase 1 registration study evaluating avapritinib in patients with PDGFRA D842V-mutant GIST. In 43 evaluable PDGFRA D842V patients, avapritinib demonstrated an unprecedented ORR of 91% with CR rate of 9% and median DOR not reached at 27 months. ctDNA clearance rate was 98%. PDGFRA D842V is intrinsically resistant to imatinib, sunitinib, and regorafenib. Avapritinib binds the active conformation of the PDGFRA kinase. Key adverse events included periorbital edema, nausea, and cognitive effects. NAVIGATOR led to FDA approval of avapritinib for PDGFRA exon 18 mutant GIST, transforming a previously untreatable molecular subtype.
title: A Study of BLU-285 in Patients With GIST and Other Relapsed or Refractory Solid Tumors (NAVIGATOR)
biomarker_criteria: PDGFRA D842V mutation or other PDGFRA exon 18 mutations

text_summary: KEYNOTE-826 is a phase 3, randomized, double-blind trial comparing pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy (cisplatin or carboplatin plus paclitaxel, with or without bevacizumab) versus placebo plus chemotherapy in 617 patients with persistent, recurrent, or metastatic cervical cancer. Pembrolizumab improved PFS (10.4 vs 8.2 months, HR 0.65) and OS (24-month OS 53% vs 41.7%, HR 0.64). ORR was 68.1% versus 50.2%. Benefit was observed regardless of PD-L1 CPS status. KEYNOTE-826 established pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy as the new first-line standard for advanced cervical cancer.
title: Pembrolizumab Plus Chemotherapy in Persistent, Recurrent, or Metastatic Cervical Cancer (KEYNOTE-826)
biomarker_criteria: All-comers (PD-L1 CPS stratified but benefit across subgroups)
interventions: pembrolizumab + chemotherapy +/- bevacizumab vs placebo + chemotherapy

text_summary: KEYNOTE-048 is a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial evaluating pembrolizumab monotherapy and pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy versus cetuximab plus chemotherapy (EXTREME regimen) in 882 patients with previously untreated recurrent or metastatic HNSCC. In PD-L1 CPS>=20, pembrolizumab monotherapy demonstrated superior OS (14.9 vs 10.7 months, HR 0.61). In CPS>=1, pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy showed superior OS (13.0 vs 10.7 months, HR 0.65). KEYNOTE-048 established a PD-L1-based treatment algorithm: pembrolizumab monotherapy for CPS>=20, pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy for CPS>=1, and cetuximab-based chemotherapy for CPS<1.
title: Pembrolizumab Alone or With Chemotherapy vs Cetuximab With Chemotherapy in Recurrent/Metastatic HNSCC (KEYNOTE-048)
cancer_types: head and neck squamous cell carcinoma

text_summary: RUBY (ENGOT-EN6-NSGO/GOG-3031) is a phase 3, randomized, double-blind trial comparing dostarlimab plus chemotherapy (carboplatin-paclitaxel) followed by dostarlimab maintenance versus placebo plus chemotherapy in 494 patients with primary advanced or first recurrence of endometrial cancer. In the dMMR/MSI-H subpopulation (n=118), dostarlimab dramatically improved PFS (not reached vs 7.7 months, HR 0.28) and 24-month PFS rate (61.4% vs 15.7%). In the overall population, PFS HR was 0.64. RUBY established front-line immunotherapy plus chemotherapy as the new standard for advanced endometrial cancer, particularly in dMMR tumors.
title: Dostarlimab Plus Carboplatin-Paclitaxel vs Placebo Plus Chemotherapy in Endometrial Cancer (RUBY)

text_summary: NRG-GY018 is a phase 3, randomized, double-blind trial evaluating pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy (carboplatin-paclitaxel) followed by pembrolizumab maintenance versus placebo plus chemotherapy in 816 patients with measurable stage III/IVA/IVB or recurrent endometrial carcinoma. In the dMMR cohort, pembrolizumab dramatically improved PFS (not reached vs 7.6 months, HR 0.30). Critically, in the pMMR cohort (75% of patients), pembrolizumab also significantly improved PFS (13.1 vs 8.7 months, HR 0.54), demonstrating benefit beyond the dMMR population. NRG-GY018 established front-line immunotherapy-chemotherapy across endometrial cancer subtypes.
title: Pembrolizumab Plus Chemotherapy in Endometrial Cancer (NRG-GY018)
biomarker_criteria: dMMR and pMMR (benefit in both, greatest in dMMR)

text_summary: RESONATE is a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing ibrutinib with ofatumumab in 391 patients with relapsed or refractory CLL/SLL who had received at least one prior therapy. Ibrutinib dramatically improved PFS (median not reached vs 8.1 months, HR 0.22) and OS (1-year OS 90% vs 81%, HR 0.43). Benefit was observed across high-risk subgroups including del(17p)/TP53 mutation and unmutated IGHV. At 6-year follow-up, median PFS was 44.1 months. Key adverse events included atrial fibrillation (11%), bleeding, and diarrhea. RESONATE established ibrutinib as a transformative therapy for relapsed CLL.
title: Ibrutinib Versus Ofatumumab in Previously Treated CLL/SLL (RESONATE)

text_summary: ALPINE is a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing zanubrutinib with ibrutinib in 652 patients with relapsed/refractory CLL/SLL. Zanubrutinib demonstrated superior ORR (80.4% vs 72.9%) and a trend toward improved PFS (HR 0.65). Importantly, zanubrutinib showed significantly lower rates of atrial fibrillation/flutter (2.5% vs 10.1%) and major hemorrhage (3.4% vs 5.5%), attributed to greater BTK selectivity and less off-target kinase inhibition. ALPINE established zanubrutinib as a BTK inhibitor with improved cardiac safety profile and led to FDA approval for CLL/SLL.
title: Zanubrutinib Versus Ibrutinib in Relapsed/Refractory CLL/SLL (ALPINE)

text_summary: POLO is a phase 3, randomized, double-blind trial comparing olaparib maintenance with placebo in 154 patients with germline BRCA1/2-mutant metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma whose disease had not progressed during first-line platinum-based chemotherapy. Olaparib significantly improved PFS (7.4 vs 3.8 months, HR 0.53, p=0.004). However, the final OS analysis did not show significant difference (19.0 vs 19.2 months). POLO established olaparib as the first targeted and first maintenance therapy for pancreatic cancer, though the lack of OS benefit tempered enthusiasm.
title: Olaparib Maintenance in Patients With Germline BRCA-Mutated Metastatic Pancreatic Cancer (POLO)

text_summary: CheckMate-743 is a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing nivolumab plus ipilimumab with platinum-based chemotherapy (cisplatin or carboplatin plus pemetrexed) in 605 patients with previously untreated, unresectable malignant pleural mesothelioma. The combination demonstrated improved OS (18.1 vs 14.1 months, HR 0.73, p=0.002). The benefit was particularly pronounced in epithelioid histology (HR 0.86) and non-epithelioid histology (HR 0.46), where chemotherapy outcomes are especially poor. ORR was 40% versus 43%, but responses to immunotherapy were more durable. CheckMate-743 established dual checkpoint blockade as the first-line standard for unresectable mesothelioma, the first new first-line regimen in 17 years.
title: Nivolumab Plus Ipilimumab Versus Chemotherapy as First-Line Treatment in Unresectable Pleural Mesothelioma (CheckMate-743)

text_summary: PROSPER is a phase 3, randomized, double-blind trial evaluating enzalutamide plus ADT versus placebo plus ADT in 1,401 patients with nonmetastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer with PSA doubling time <=10 months. Enzalutamide improved metastasis-free survival (36.6 vs 14.7 months, HR 0.29) and OS (67.0 vs 56.3 months, HR 0.73, p=0.001). PROSPER established next-generation AR inhibitors as standard of care in nmCRPC, demonstrating that delaying metastatic progression translates into OS benefit.
title: Enzalutamide in Nonmetastatic Castration-Resistant Prostate Cancer (PROSPER)

text_summary: CLEAR (KEYNOTE-581) is a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab, lenvatinib plus everolimus, and sunitinib in 1,069 patients with previously untreated advanced clear cell RCC. Lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab demonstrated superior PFS (23.9 vs 9.2 months, HR 0.39), OS (HR 0.66), and ORR (71% vs 36.1%) compared with sunitinib. The CR rate was 16.1%. The PFS and ORR represented the highest efficacy in first-line mRCC trials. Frequent dose modifications were needed for diarrhea, hypertension, and hypothyroidism. CLEAR positioned lenvatinib plus pembrolizumab as a leading first-line option for mRCC.
title: Lenvatinib Plus Pembrolizumab or Everolimus Versus Sunitinib in Advanced RCC (CLEAR/KEYNOTE-581)
interventions: lenvatinib + pembrolizumab vs lenvatinib + everolimus vs sunitinib

text_summary: CheckMate-141 is a phase 3, randomized, open-label trial comparing nivolumab with investigator's choice therapy (methotrexate, docetaxel, or cetuximab) in 361 patients with recurrent HNSCC who had progressed within 6 months of platinum-based chemotherapy. Nivolumab improved OS (7.5 vs 5.1 months, HR 0.70) with 1-year OS rate of 36% versus 17%. Responses were durable (median DOR not reached). Benefit was observed regardless of HPV status. CheckMate-141 established nivolumab as the first approved immunotherapy for second-line HNSCC.
title: Nivolumab Versus Investigator's Choice in Recurrent or Metastatic HNSCC (CheckMate-141)
cancer_types: head and neck squamous cell carcinoma
biomarker_criteria: All-comers (HPV and PD-L1 status assessed but not required)
interventions: nivolumab vs investigator's choice (methotrexate, docetaxel, or cetuximab)
